Name: spectral_quantization_aware_training
Title: Spectral Quantization-Aware Training for Efficient and Robust Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating spectral quantization awareness into the training process can lead to more efficient and robust neural networks. By dynamically allocating numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry, we can reduce the computational cost and memory usage of neural networks while maintaining their accuracy.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) and Geometric Signal Separation, which demonstrate the effectiveness of using spectral methods for efficient and robust neural network inference. However, our proposal differs from these works in that we focus on incorporating spectral quantization awareness into the training process, rather than just inference. This allows us to optimize the neural network's architecture and weights for efficient and robust quantization, leading to better performance and efficiency.
Abstract: We propose a novel training framework that incorporates spectral quantization awareness into the training process. Our framework dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry, allowing us to reduce the computational cost and memory usage of neural networks while maintaining their accuracy. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework on a range of benchmarks, including image classification and language modeling tasks. Our results show that our framework can achieve significant reductions in computational cost and memory usage while maintaining state-of-the-art accuracy. We also provide theoretical bounds linking spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise, establishing a transparent and reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on a range of benchmarks, including image classification and language modeling tasks. We will evaluate the performance of our framework in terms of accuracy, computational cost, and memory usage, and compare it to state-of-the-art baselines. We will also conduct ablation studies to investigate the effectiveness of different components of our framework, such as the spectral quantization-aware loss function and the dynamic precision allocation mechanism.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is that our framework may not be effective for all types of neural networks or tasks. Additionally, our framework may require significant modifications to the training process, which could be challenging to implement in practice. To mitigate these risks, we will conduct thorough experiments and ablation studies to evaluate the effectiveness of our framework and identify potential limitations.
